brief_document.title: Worker heartbeats and lost-worker handling
brief_document.purpose: Keep long-running remote activities alive and detect dead workers. A worker heartbeats over its stream so the engine knows its task is still running; a worker that goes silent or disconnects before reporting has its in-flight task surfaced to the engine as failed, leaving the retry decision to the engine.
brief_document.task: Implement heartbeat handling on the worker stream: track per-task liveness against a configured heartbeat window, accept Heartbeat messages (with optional progress), and when a worker misses its window or disconnects before reporting, mark it lost, remove it from the registry, and surface its in-flight task(s) to the engine as failed so the engine applies its retry policy. The window is configured, not hardcoded.

requirements.title: Heartbeat tracking
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL accept Heartbeat messages on the worker stream, recording per-task liveness against a configured heartbeat window, and SHALL accept optional progress in a heartbeat without treating it as a result.
acceptance: heartbeat.rs tracks, per in-flight task, the time of the last heartbeat and the configured heartbeat window (from AW-006 config, no hardcoded default — CO11).
A received Heartbeat (AW-005) refreshes the task's liveness; optional progress is recorded for observability but is not an ActivityResult and does not complete the activity.
A test feeds heartbeats and asserts a task stays live across the window.

requirements.title: Lost-worker detection and failure surfacing
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL mark a worker lost when it misses its heartbeat window or disconnects before reporting, remove it from the registry, and surface its in-flight task(s) to the engine as failed, leaving the retry decision to the engine.
acceptance: When a worker's last heartbeat is older than the configured window, or its stream disconnects with in-flight tasks, the worker is removed from the registry and each in-flight task is surfaced to the engine as a failed activity (D9).
The failure is surfaced via the engine's activity contract (as in AW-011 R3); the server makes no retry decision itself — the engine applies its retry/timeout policy (aion-engine D7, CO6).
The surfaced failure carries a terminal-or-retryable classification consistent with a lost worker (documented), so the engine's policy behaves correctly.

A test simulates both a missed-heartbeat timeout and an abrupt disconnect with an in-flight task, asserting the worker is deregistered and the engine's activity-failure path is invoked exactly once per in-flight task.

boundaries: SHALL NOT decide or perform activity retries — that is the engine's (D9, CO6).
SHALL NOT build the worker-side heartbeat sender — that is cluster AR.
SHALL NOT hardcode the heartbeat window (CO11).
verification: Time-dependent tests inject the clock; no wall-clock sleeps in tests.
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: A worker that misses its heartbeat window or disconnects before reporting is marked lost and its in-flight task is surfaced to the engine as failed, leaving the retry decision to the engine.

stories.text: As a remote worker running a long activity, I want to heartbeat so that the engine knows I am alive and does not treat my task as failed.

constraints.text: No #[allow]/#[expect]/#[ignore] lint bypasses per CLAUDE.md; tests needing a running server gate at runtime (a *_TEST_URL env var with a logged skip), never #[ignore].
lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
aion-proto is a leaf crate: it depends on aion-core and external crates only — no aion, no aion-server, no store backend. Structural; must hold (per D2).
aion-server depends on aion, aion-proto, and a store backend among workspace crates; it contains no workflow execution logic — it delegates every durable decision to the Engine (per D5).
Wire data types (proto messages, streamed events, summaries) derive Serialize + Deserialize; config types that carry secrets are Deserialize-only (D4, project credential policy).
Library errors use thiserror (WireError in proto, ServerError in server); anyhow appears only in aion-server's top-level main. No unwrap/expect in library code; lock/stream poison handled explicitly.
No cross-namespace operation reaches the engine: namespace authorisation and scoping happen at the adapter boundary before any Engine call (per D8).
A slow or stalled WebSocket consumer is dropped with a typed lag error; per-connection buffers are bounded; the engine broadcast is never back-pressured by a subscriber (per D6).
No hardcoded operational defaults (ports, heartbeat window, buffer bound, timeouts): all from config; engine-owned defaults deferred to the engine (per CLAUDE.md 'no assumed defaults').
The wire Event/WorkflowFilter/WorkflowSummary/WorkflowStatus and ID types are aion-core's types, not re-declared wire clones (per D2).

resolved_context.intention: Build the network skin of Aion. The aion engine crate is transport-agnostic by design — no HTTP, gRPC, or WebSocket — so a CLI embeds it with nothing but a file-backed store. Teams running many services want a managed deployment: one binary clients and workers point at, that streams live workflow events to a dashboard, isolates tenants, and dispatches activity tasks to out-of-process workers in any language. That is aion-server, and the wire contract it speaks is aion-proto. aion-proto is a pure leaf-level contract — gRPC service definitions plus serde wire types — that the server, every client SDK (AL), and every worker SDK (AR) agree on, defined once so they never drift. aion-server is the deployable: it wraps a live Engine, translates wire requests into Engine calls, tails the engine's event broadcast to feed WebSocket subscribers, runs the server side of the remote-worker protocol, partitions everything by namespace, and serves the dashboard's API and static assets. It adds no execution logic — every durable decision still happens in aion.
